column1: What is the primary purpose of a Version Control System (VCS)?
column2: To record changes to files over time so specific versions can be recalled later.

column1: Which specific local VCS tool works by keeping patch sets in a special format on disk?
How does a Centralized Version Control System (CVCS) handle file storage?
column2: A single server contains all versioned files and clients check out files from that central place.

column1: What are two examples of Centralized Version Control Systems (CVCS) mentioned in the text?
What is the main risk associated with the single point of failure in a CVCS?
column2: If the central database is corrupted without backups, the entire history of the project is lost.

column1: How does a Distributed Version Control System (DVCS) differ from a CVCS regarding repository access?
column2: Clients fully mirror the repository, including its full history, rather than just checking out the latest snapshot.

column1: What allows a DVCS to restore a dead server using a client repository?
column2: Every client clone is a full backup of all the repository data.

column1: In what year was Git developed?
Who was the primary creator of Git?
What event prompted the Linux development community to create Git?
column2: The breakdown of the relationship with BitKeeper and the revocation of its free-of-charge status.

column1: Which Git design goal focuses on the ability to handle thousands of parallel branches?
Concept: Delta-based version control
column2: Definition: Systems that store information as a base version of each file plus a list of file-based changes over time.

column1: How does Git's conceptual data model differ from delta-based systems?
column2: Git thinks of its data as a series of snapshots of a miniature filesystem.

column1: How does Git handle files that have not changed between commits to save space?
column2: It stores a link to the previous identical file it has already stored rather than storing the file again.

column1: Why are most Git operations nearly instantaneous compared to CVCS operations?
column2: Most operations use only local files and resources from the local database on disk.

column1: What mechanism does Git use to ensure data integrity and detect file corruption?
A SHA-1 hash in Git is a character string of what length?
What character set is used to compose a SHA-1 hash string?
Git refers to objects in its database primarily by their _____ rather than by file name.
In Git, the state where a file has been changed but not yet committed is called _____.
In Git, the state where a modified file is marked in its current version for the next commit is called _____.
In Git, the state where data is safely stored in the local database is called _____.
Which Git section contains a single checkout of one version of the project for the user to modify?
What is the technical name for the staging area in Git?
Which Git section stores the metadata and object database and is copied during a clone?
Term: Git clone
column2: Definition: The process of copying a full version of nearly all data the server has to a local machine.

column1: Which command is used to install Git on a Fedora-based Linux system?
What tool allows users to set configuration variables that control Git's look and operation?
Which configuration file applies settings to every user on the system?
Which flag is used with $\sh{git config}$ to write settings to the system-wide configuration file?
Where is the user-specific global configuration file located on most systems?
Which configuration file level takes the highest priority and overrides all others?
column2: The local config file in the .git directory of the specific repository.

column1: What command sets the global user name for Git commits?
What command sets the global email address for Git commits?
Which configuration key allows you to change the default initial branch name from 'master'?
What command displays all settings Git finds along with the files they originated from?
How can you get a concise "help" summary of options for the $\sh{git add}$ command?
What does the command $\sh{git init}$ do in an existing project directory?
column2: It creates a .git subdirectory containing the necessary repository skeleton files.

column1: In Git, what is the difference between "tracked" and "untracked" files?
column2: Tracked files were in the last snapshot or are staged; untracked files are everything else in the working directory.

column1: Which command is the primary tool for determining which files are in which state?
What is the result of running $\sh{git add [file]}$ on a new, untracked file?
column2: The file becomes tracked and is staged to be committed.

column1: How does $git add$ behave when a directory path is provided as an argument?
column2: It adds all files in that directory recursively.

column1: If you modify a file after running $git add$ but before committing, what must you do to include the latest changes?
column2: Run $git add$ again to stage the latest version of the file.

column1: Which transfer protocol uses the format $\sh{user@server:path/to/repo.git}$?
Under what condition will $git status$ report "nothing to commit, working tree clean"?
column2: When none of the tracked files are modified and there are no untracked files.
